The Blink: Nietzsche's Theory of Deliberate Blindness

2 min read · 490 words · Deep Dive #105 · 2026-04-09
Source: Thus Spoke Zarathustra — Friedrich Nietzsche

"We have invented happiness," say the Last Men. And they blink.

That blink is the most loaded gesture in Nietzsche. It's not confusion. It's not sleepiness. It's the deliberate closing of eyes that could see further. The Last Man blinks because looking clearly would mean seeing what he's given up — and that's a view he cannot afford.

Nietzsche deploys the blink five times in the Last Man passage of Zarathustra, each time after a statement of false contentment. "We have invented happiness" — blink. "Everyone wants the same, everyone is the same" — blink. The rhythm is nauseating by design. Each blink is a micro-capitulation, a tiny voluntary death of awareness.

The key word is voluntary. The Last Man isn't stupid. He's not incapable of seeing greatness, risk, or possibility. He chooses not to see them because seeing would create an obligation to act. Awareness is expensive — it demands response. The blink is the refusal to pay that price. It's the philosophy of "I'd rather not know."

Atlas Shrugged is full of blinkers. The politicians who "don't understand" why factories close. The journalists who "can't explain" why innovation stops. The academics who find it "puzzling" that talent leaves. None of them are actually confused. They're performing confusion because the alternative — admitting that their policies caused the collapse — would require them to change. And change is what the Last Man exists to avoid.

The modern version of the blink is the phrase "it's more nuanced than that." Not because nuance is bad — it's essential — but because "nuance" has become the socially acceptable way to blink at clear truths. Someone builds something great? "Well, it's more nuanced — what about the externalities?" Someone fails spectacularly through obvious laziness? "Well, it's more nuanced — what about systemic factors?" The nuance-blink dissolves all distinction between excellence and mediocrity into a comfortable grey.

Nietzsche's blink is also a social contagion. "Everyone wants the same, everyone is the same" — the Last Man doesn't just blink for himself, he demands that others blink too. The person who keeps their eyes open is threatening not because they see more, but because their open eyes remind the blinkers of what they chose to close. This is why Dagny is hated — not for what she does, but for what her existence reveals about those who don't.

The antidote to the blink isn't just seeing clearly. It's refusing to pretend you don't see. That's harder than it sounds. The social pressure to blink — to call things complicated when they're simple, to call mediocrity acceptable when it's chosen, to call comfort enough when it isn't — is the ambient air of the Last Man's world. Keeping your eyes open is, in Nietzsche's framework, the first act of self-overcoming.
